preFix.title: Sourcing replacement stock after a disease outbreak
preFix.subtitle: Movement restrictions, standstill periods, and finding healthy replacements after losing animals.
preFix.excerpt: Losing livestock to disease is one of the harder experiences on a smallholding. What comes next involves legal restrictions on movement, a fallow period before restocking, and sourcing animals whose health history you can verify. This reading covers the practical and regulatory sequence from outbreak to restocked holding.
preFix.sourceNotes:
DEFRA, Notifiable animal diseases and reporting obligations. https://www.gov.uk/guidance/notifiable-diseases-in-animals
AHDB Beef and Lamb, Biosecurity and restocking guidance.
APHA, Animal movement restrictions guidance. https://www.gov.uk/guidance/animal-movements
Cobbett, Cottage Economy (1822), Project Gutenberg. Historical perspective on smallholding recovery.

content.text: A disease outbreak on a smallholding leaves three immediate questions. What do I need to report to APHA? When can I bring in new animals? How do I avoid the same thing happening again? The practical answers depend on whether the disease is notifiable, and on which species were affected.
Most conditions a small-scale keeper encounters are not
. You manage them yourself or with vet input, then restock when ready. The section below covers what to do when the disease is notifiable.

Most disease losses you will face are not notifiable. Coccidiosis in lambs, snuffles in rabbits, and PRRS in pigs are all examples. You deal with them via your vet, note the event in your register, and restock when ready. The steps below are good practice but are not required by law for these cases.
You must tell APHA the same day you think you have a notifiable disease. Do not move or dispose of animals while you wait. Call APHA on 0300 020 0301 for advice on testing and next steps.
Key ones for smallholders are foot and mouth disease, avian flu, Newcastle disease, swine fever, and blue tongue.

Before bringing in replacement animals, clean and disinfect all housing, feeders, drinkers, and handling equipment. The order matters: remove all organic matter before applying disinfectant. Disinfectant does not penetrate a layer of dried manure; all it does is sterilise the surface. Pressure-wash first, allow to dry, then apply an approved disinfectant at the stated rate.

For poultry disease. Leave the building empty and dry for at least two weeks after disinfection before new birds arrive. For pig housing following a bacterial or viral event, four weeks empty is safer. The fallow period lets residual pathogens die back.

New stock arriving on the holding triggers the movement standstill clock: six days for sheep, cattle. And goats; 20 days for pigs. No animal of that species may leave the holding until the standstill period expires. If you receive animals in two separate batches, the second batch resets the clock.

The best replacement animals come from a single source that you have visited and assessed. Not from a market where the animal's previous history is unknown. Ask the vendor about their vaccination programme. Any disease history in the flock or herd in the past 12 months. And what the animals were eating before arrival. Animals from a market may have mixed with many other holdings and are higher risk.

Your CPH number is required before any sheep, goats, cattle, or pigs can be moved onto the holding. If the holding has been empty for a period and you are not sure whether your CPH is still active. Check with the Rural Payments Agency before arranging the delivery.

attrs.body: Introduce new animals into a separate pen or paddock away from any existing stock for at least two weeks. Even if they came from a health-checked source. Even a healthy-looking animal from a reputable holding gets a quarantine period. Use this time to check for signs of disease, check for parasites. And let the animals settle before mixing.
attrs.title: Quarantining new arrivals

content.text: Record the disease event and the restocking date in your holding register. Alongside the source of the new animals and what treatments were given. For sheep and goats, the on-farm medicine book must record any prescription treatments. For pigs, the movement documentation from the seller must be retained for three years. Good records are the baseline for the next decision if disease recurs.

Take a moment before restocking to consider whether the original biosecurity on the holding was adequate. Common gaps are a gate with no footbath, shared equipment between species. Or animals bought from markets without quarantine. Each gap costs nothing to close; the cost of a second outbreak is much higher.
